channel.title: Nature Physics
channel.description: Nature Physics offers news and reviews alongside top-quality research papers in a monthly publication, covering the entire spectrum of physics. Physics addresses the properties and interactions of matter and energy, and plays a key role in the development of a broad range of technologies. To reflect this, Nature Physics covers all areas of pure and applied physics research. The journal focuses on core physics disciplines, but is also open to a broad range of topics whose central theme falls within the bounds of physics.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: When percolation triggers fatigue
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03210-w

Everyday objects often fail from repeated stress. A study shows that fatigue failure in glasses is governed by damage percolation and predictable from early-cycle energy dissipation.
item.dc:title: When percolation triggers fatigue
item.dc:creator: David Richard

item.title: Geometric origin of particle and dislocation dynamics during grain boundary migration
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-025-03165-4

Grain boundaries in polycrystalline systems display complicated dynamics. Now, a general framework is presented that predicts the microscopic dynamics of both particles and dislocations underlying grain boundary migration in two-dimensional colloids.
item.dc:title: Geometric origin of particle and dislocation dynamics during grain boundary migration
item.dc:creator: Berend van der Meer
Mathieu G. Baltussen
François A. Lavergne
Arran Curran
Marjolein Dijkstra
Roel P. A. Dullens

item.title: Transient grating spectroscopy down to the atomic length scale
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03203-9

Free-electron lasers generate intense, femtosecond and sub-nanometre wavelength pulses. Incorporating such X-ray light into transient grating spectroscopy reveals electron dynamics at the nanometre length scale.
item.dc:title: Transient grating spectroscopy down to the atomic length scale
item.dc:creator: Martin Beye

item.title: Kondo with a topological twist
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03209-3

In a semiconductor bilayer system, local moments in one layer interact with itinerant carriers in the other to realize a two-dimensional topological Kondo insulator.
item.dc:title: Kondo with a topological twist
item.dc:creator: Benjamin E. Feldman

item.title: The coarsening of biomimetic condensates in an active fluid is non-self-similar
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03191-w

A study of condensates that mimic biological ones within a reconstituted cytoskeleton reveals the mechanisms underlying the coarsening of the condensate.
item.dc:title: The coarsening of biomimetic condensates in an active fluid is non-self-similar
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Laprade
Layne B. Frechette
Christopher Amey
Adrielle T. Cusi
Aparna Baskaran
W. Benjamin Rogers
Guillaume Duclos

item.title: High-field triplet superconductivity in a transition metal dichalcogenide superlattice
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03185-8

BaTa2S5, which consists of alternating layers of TaS2 and Ba2TaS4, is shown to host a triplet superconducting phase at high magnetic field. This triplet phase emerges from another—more conventional—superconducting state.
item.dc:title: High-field triplet superconductivity in a transition metal dichalcogenide superlattice
item.dc:creator: S. Y. Frank Zhao
Paul M. Neves
Joshua P. Wakefield
Shiang Fang
Alan Chen
Johanna C. Palmstrom
David E. Graf
Avi Auslender
David C. Bell
Pavel A. Volkov
Takehito Suzuki
Joseph G. Checkelsky

item.title: Cavity-enhanced spectroscopy in the deep cryogenic regime for quantum sensing and metrology
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03204-8

Operating cavity-based spectrometers at low temperature has several advantages, such as improved sensitivity. Now, a cavity-enhanced spectrometer is demonstrated down to 4 K.
item.dc:title: Cavity-enhanced spectroscopy in the deep cryogenic regime for quantum sensing and metrology
item.dc:creator: K. Stankiewicz
M. Makowski
M. Słowiński
K. L. Sołtys
B. Bednarski
H. J. Jóźwiak
N. Stolarczyk
M. Narożnik
D. Kierski
S. Wójtewicz
A. Cygan
G. Kowzan
P. Masłowski
M. Piwiński
D. Lisak
P. Wcisło

item.title: Logical multi-qubit entanglement with dual-rail superconducting qubits
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03211-9

Quantum computers often exhibit a bias in the type of error that is the most common or severe. Entanglement has now been demonstrated for qubits encoded with an error correction code that is designed to efficiently handle biased errors.
item.dc:title: Logical multi-qubit entanglement with dual-rail superconducting qubits
item.dc:creator: Wenhui Huang
Xuandong Sun
Jiawei Zhang
Zechen Guo
Peisheng Huang
Yongqi Liang
Yiting Liu
